name: T Townsend Brown
description: T Townsend Brown (1905-1985) was an American physicist known for his research into electrostatics and electrogravitics. He is most famous for his claims of discovering a phenomenon he called the Biefeld-Brown effect, where high-voltage electricity applied to an asymmetric capacitor allegedly produces a thrust. Proponents claim this effect could revolutionize aerospace propulsion, leading to silent, fuel-free flight and space travel. Brown demonstrated his devices, sometimes called 'lifters,' which appeared to defy gravity. The origins of his work trace back to the 1920s. However, mainstream physics attributes any observed movement to ionic wind—the movement of air molecules ionized by the high-voltage discharge. This explanation posits that the thrust is not a novel gravitational effect but a conventional electrostatic phenomenon. Brown's work has become a focus of alternative propulsion research and conspiracy theories, often linked to suppressed technologies and government secrecy. While experiments seemingly replicate the effect, the consensus remains that ionic wind is the primary driving force, and no evidence supports the claim of a genuine anti-gravity or electrogravitic effect.
subtitle: Electrogravitics and the Biefeld-Brown Effect

claims.claim: The Biefeld-Brown effect demonstrates a direct link between electricity and gravity.
claims.evidence: Observed thrust in asymmetric capacitors under high voltage.
claims.criticism: The observed thrust is explained by ionic wind, a well-understood electrostatic phenomenon. Controlled experiments show performance consistent with ionic wind propulsion.

claims.claim: The Biefeld-Brown effect can be used for silent, fuel-free propulsion.
claims.evidence: Demonstrations of 'lifters' that appear to defy gravity.
claims.criticism: Lifters are inefficient and only function within an atmosphere due to the requirement of air for ionic wind. They are not a viable alternative to conventional propulsion in vacuum.

claims.claim: The true nature of the Biefeld-Brown effect has been suppressed by governments and corporations.
claims.evidence: Lack of funding for research and development, claims of classified research.
claims.criticism: The lack of funding is due to the effect's low efficiency and the well-established scientific explanation of ionic wind. No credible evidence supports claims of suppression.

keyFigures.name: T Townsend Brown
keyFigures.role: Discoverer and proponent of the Biefeld-Brown effect

keyFigures.name: Paul Schatzkin
keyFigures.role: Researcher and advocate of the Biefeld-Brown effect

keyFigures.name: Thomas Valone
keyFigures.role: Critic of electrogravitics claims, emphasizing ionic wind explanation

mythologyConnections: Flying carpets (alleged resemblance to antigravity devices)
Myths of levitation and flight through magical means

timeline.event: T Townsend Brown begins experimenting with high-voltage devices.
Brown files his first patent related to electrogravitics.
Brown conducts demonstrations of his devices for the military and media.
Continued interest and research into the Biefeld-Brown effect, primarily among enthusiasts and alternative science researchers.
